title: Points ignition failure and conversion — pre-1980 models
description: Early CB550/650/750 models use breaker points ignition that require regular adjustment and replacement. Points pit, gap changes, timing drifts, and the advance mechanism sticks. Electronic ignition conversions eliminate all of these problems permanently.
causes: Points contact pitting from normal arcing
Condenser failure (causes excessive arcing)
Advance mechanism sticking from old grease
Points gap drifting from rubbing block wear
fix_procedure: 1. If engine runs rough or won't start: check points gap first. Open the points cover (left side of engine). Gap should be 0.3-0.4mm. 2. If points are pitted or burned: replace points and condenser as a set. 3. Check advance mechanism: remove it and check that the weights move freely. Clean and re-grease with light grease. 4. Set timing with a strobe light after points adjustment. 5. Better long-term solution: install electronic ignition conversion (Dyna, Pamco, or Boyer Bransden). 6. Forum tip: if you're keeping a vintage Honda as a rider (not a concours show bike), electronic ignition conversion is the single best reliability upgrade. The Pamco ignition for CB750 SOHC ($150) is plug-and-play, never needs adjustment, and makes the bike start like a modern machine. Points were designed to be serviced every 3000 miles — that's not practical for a daily rider. Also, the original condensers are NLA (no longer available) from Honda — aftermarket condensers are hit-or-miss quality. Going electronic eliminates the condenser entirely.
parts_needed: Electronic ignition kit (Pamco, Dyna, or Boyer)
Points and condenser set (if keeping points)

title: Carburetor bank rebuild — 4-carb inline-4 service
description: Every vintage Honda inline-4 with 30+ year old carbs needs a full rebuild. The Keihin carbs use rubber diaphragms, float valve needles, and tiny pilot jets that all deteriorate with age and ethanol fuel. A carb bank rebuild on a CB750 is a 4-6 hour job but transforms a non-running bike into a reliable runner.
causes: Pilot jet clogging from ethanol fuel varnish
Slide diaphragm cracks from age/UV
Float valve needle wear causing flooding
Main jet clogging from fuel residue
O-ring deterioration causing vacuum leaks
fix_procedure: 1. Remove carb bank (4 carbs as a unit). Document all hose connections with photos. 2. Disassemble each carb completely: float bowl, main jet, pilot jet, needle, slide, diaphragm. 3. Soak all metal parts in carb cleaner overnight. 4. Blow every passage with compressed air — especially pilot circuit passages. 5. Replace all rubber parts: diaphragms, O-rings, float valve needles. 6. Set float height per service manual. 7. Reassemble, reinstall, sync with vacuum gauges. 8. Forum tip: buy a complete rebuild kit for all 4 carbs (K&L Supply or Keyster, $50-80). Don't try to clean old rubber parts — replace everything rubber. The CB750 SOHC carbs are the most documented carbs in motorcycle history — hundreds of rebuild guides exist. The most common mistake is not cleaning the pilot air bleed passage (tiny hole in the carb throat, easy to miss). If one cylinder doesn't idle after rebuild, it's usually the pilot air bleed. Also, bench-sync the carbs before installing — adjust the butterfly plates to crack open at the same throttle position.

title: Charging system failure — early Honda generators and stators
description: Vintage Hondas use two different charging systems: generators (CB750 K0-K1) and stators (CB750 K2+ and all CB550/650). Both fail from age, heat, and insulation breakdown. The voltage regulator is a separate external box that also fails. Symptom: battery dies while riding, lights dim progressively.
causes: Stator winding insulation breakdown from 40+ years of heat
Regulator/rectifier failure
Rotor magnet weakening from age
Brush wear on generator models
Corroded connections reducing charging efficiency
fix_procedure: 1. Test: engine at 3000 RPM, voltmeter across battery. Should be 13.5-14.5V. 2. If low: test stator AC output at the stator connector. All legs should produce equal AC voltage. 3. Test regulator: if stator is good but DC output is wrong, regulator has failed. 4. Replace stator with modern aftermarket (Rick's, Electrosport). 5. Replace regulator with MOSFET type. 6. Check all ground connections — corroded grounds kill charging on vintage bikes. 7. Forum tip: the vintage Honda charging system is adequate for STOCK electrical loads — headlight, tail light, and ignition. If you've added any accessories (USB charger, louder horn, LED lights), you may have exceeded the charging capacity. The CB750 SOHC stator puts out about 250W — that's barely enough. A modern MOSFET regulator is more efficient than the OEM regulator and recovers 10-15% of lost charging capacity. Also, if you're rebuilding a vintage Honda, clean every ground point to bare metal — 40 years of corrosion on ground connections is the hidden killer of vintage Honda electrical systems.

title: Cam chain and tensioner wear — high-mileage SOHC engines
description: The SOHC Honda engines (CB750, CB550, CB650) use a cam chain driven off the crankshaft with a mechanical or automatic tensioner. After 40+ years and 30K+ miles, the chain stretches, the tensioner is maxed out, and the chain slaps. Severe wear causes valve timing retardation and power loss.
causes: Cam chain stretch from 40+ years of use
Tensioner blade/slipper wear
Tensioner spring fatigue
Oil starvation from clogged oil gallery
fix_procedure: 1. Listen for cam chain rattle — loudest at idle, changes with RPM. 2. Check tensioner adjustment: on the CB750 SOHC, the tensioner is on the right side of the cylinders. If the adjuster is maxed out, the chain needs replacement. 3. Cam chain replacement on the CB750 requires splitting the crankcase OR using an endless chain tool (grinds a link, feeds new chain through, re-rivets). 4. Replace tensioner blade/slipper and spring at the same time. 5. Forum tip: the endless chain replacement method saves you from a full engine teardown — it's the standard approach for vintage Honda cam chains. The tool grinds a link on the old chain, you connect the new chain to the old one, pull it through, then rivet the new chain closed. DID makes the correct chains. The CB750 SOHC tensioner slipper is a known wear item — replace it even if it looks OK. A worn slipper can't keep tension on the chain and the noise returns in 5K miles.
parts_needed: Cam chain (DID, correct pitch for model)

title: Fuel petcock vacuum diaphragm failure
description: Vintage Honda fuel petcocks use a vacuum-operated diaphragm that opens fuel flow when the engine is running (engine vacuum pulls the diaphragm). When the diaphragm deteriorates, fuel either leaks continuously (floods carbs, washes cylinder walls with fuel) or doesn't flow at all (fuel starvation). A common cause of 'won't start after sitting.'
causes: Vacuum diaphragm deterioration from age
Ethanol fuel attacking rubber diaphragm
Petcock body corrosion from tank rust
Vacuum hose cracking (no vacuum = no fuel)
fix_procedure: 1. If fuel leaks from the petcock when the engine is off: the diaphragm is ruptured. Fuel flows continuously. 2. If the bike runs on PRIME but dies on ON or RES: the diaphragm isn't opening (no vacuum getting through). Check the vacuum hose for cracks. 3. Rebuild: petcock rebuild kits are available ($15-25). Replace diaphragm, O-rings, and filter screen. 4. If petcock body is corroded: replace entire petcock. 5. Forum tip: the vacuum petcock test is simple — disconnect the fuel line from the carb, put a container under it, turn to ON, and suck on the vacuum port. Fuel should flow when you suck and stop when you release. If it flows continuously or doesn't flow at all, rebuild or replace. Also, a leaking petcock dumps fuel into the crankcase through the carbs — if your oil level is rising and the oil smells like gas, the petcock is flooding the carbs. Check this before replacing any engine seals.

title: Brake master cylinder and caliper rebuild — aging hydraulics
description: Vintage Honda disc brakes (single disc on CB750, dual disc on later models) have 40+ year old master cylinders and calipers with degraded seals, pitted bores, and contaminated fluid. Brake feel is spongy, lever travel is excessive, and in worst cases the brakes barely stop the bike.
causes: Master cylinder seal deterioration from age
Caliper piston corrosion from old fluid
Brake line expansion from rubber degradation
Fluid contamination (water absorption over decades)
fix_procedure: 1. Flush ALL old brake fluid out — it's 40+ years old and is basically water at this point. 2. Inspect master cylinder bore — if pitted, rebuild or replace. 3. Inspect caliper pistons — remove, clean, check bore. If pitted, replace caliper or re-sleeve bore. 4. Replace ALL rubber brake lines with stainless braided lines — they don't expand under pressure and give much better brake feel. 5. Rebuild master cylinder with new seals. 6. Bleed thoroughly with fresh DOT 4. 7. Forum tip: vintage Honda brakes were marginal when new — by modern standards, they're terrifying. The single disc CB750 with a drum rear needs every advantage it can get. Stainless braided brake lines ($30-50) are THE best brake upgrade — they make the stock calipers feel like they've been upgraded. Also, EBC HH sintered pads improve stopping power significantly over OEM organic pads. If the master cylinder is beyond rebuild (pitted bore), an aftermarket Nissin master cylinder ($50-80) is a direct replacement and better quality than the original.

title: Exhaust rust-through and header replacement
description: Original 4-into-4 or 4-into-1 exhaust systems on vintage Hondas are mild steel and rusted through decades ago on most survivors. Replacement options range from cheap reproduction headers to expensive stainless aftermarket systems. A rusted-through exhaust hurts performance, sounds terrible, and fails inspection in many states.
causes: 40+ years of mild steel corrosion
Road salt and moisture exposure
Exhaust gasket deterioration
Previous patch repairs failing
fix_procedure: 1. Inspect entire exhaust: headers, collector (if 4-1), mid-pipe, mufflers. Look for rust-through holes, cracks, and patched areas. 2. If headers are rusted through: replacement is the only real fix. Patching mild steel exhaust is temporary (weeks to months). 3. Options: reproduction OEM-style (Mac, Emgo — $200-400), aftermarket 4-1 (Kerker, Yoshimura vintage — $400-800), stainless steel custom ($600+). 4. Replace exhaust gaskets with new ones at every reinstall. 5. Forum tip: for a rider (not a concours show bike), a quality 4-into-1 exhaust (MAC or Delkevic) is the best value — lighter, more power, and stainless options that won't rust again. For a restoration, NOS (New Old Stock) OEM exhausts exist but are expensive ($500+ for a 4-into-4 set). The reproduction 4-into-4 systems from companies like CMS or CMSNL are adequate quality for a rider. Also, use copper or nickel anti-seize on header bolts — they'll need to come off again someday, and rusted-in header bolts on aluminum heads snap off and require helicoil repairs.

title: Fork seal leak and fork tube pitting
description: Vintage Honda fork tubes develop pitting from 40+ years of road debris impact and corrosion. Pitted tubes tear fork seals immediately after replacement, causing continuous oil weeping. Chrome plating deteriorates and flakes. The only real fix for pitted tubes is re-chroming or replacement.
causes: Fork tube surface pitting from 40+ years of debris
Chrome plating deterioration and flaking
Fork seal hardening from age
Fork oil turned to sludge from decades of neglect
fix_procedure: 1. Check fork tubes: run a fingernail down the chrome surface. Any catch = pit that will tear a seal. 2. If minor pitting: polish with 1500-grit wet/dry sandpaper — may buy 5-10K miles on a new seal. 3. If severe pitting or chrome flaking: tubes must be re-chromed ($100-150 per tube) or replaced (used from eBay or NOS). 4. Replace fork seals and dust boots. 5. Change fork oil (ATF Type F was the original spec — modern 10W fork oil is superior). 6. Forum tip: re-chroming fork tubes is actually a great investment on a vintage Honda — it costs $200-300 for the pair and gives you brand-new chrome that lasts another 20+ years. Local chrome shops do this regularly. While the tubes are out, have them checked for straightness — a bent fork tube causes uneven tire wear and handling issues. Also, the original fork oil spec on many vintage Hondas is ATF Type F — it works but modern fork oil gives much better damping. Use 10W for stock springs, 15W if you've installed progressive springs.

title: Wiring harness deterioration — brittle insulation and corroded connectors
description: Vintage Honda wiring harnesses use cloth-over-rubber insulation that becomes brittle and crumbles with age. The connectors corrode internally. The result is intermittent shorts, opens, and grounds that cause random electrical failures — lights flickering, fuses blowing, and no-start conditions.
causes: Cloth insulation becoming brittle and crumbling from age
Connector oxidation from 40+ years
Previous owners splicing with electrical tape (fails)
Heat damage from routing too close to engine
fix_procedure: 1. Inspect harness: look for bare wire showing through crumbled insulation, melted insulation near the engine, and green corrosion on connectors. 2. If damage is localized: repair individual wires with solder, heat-shrink, and proper routing. 3. If harness is extensively deteriorated: replace with a reproduction harness. 4. Clean all connectors with electrical contact cleaner, apply dielectric grease. 5. Check for prior hack wiring (electrical tape splices, wrong gauge wire, missing grounds). 6. Forum tip: reproduction wiring harnesses for CB750, CB550, and CB650 are available from Rick's Motorsport, Vintage Connections, and Revival Cycles ($150-300). They're color-coded to match the original and include modern connectors. This is one of those jobs where replacement is better than repair — chasing intermittent electrical faults in a 40-year-old cloth-wrapped harness will cost more in labor hours than a new harness costs in parts. While you're at it, upgrade to a modern fuse box (blade fuses instead of glass tube fuses).

title: Engine gasket weeping and oil leak — top end and covers
description: Every vintage Honda engine leaks oil from somewhere after 40+ years. Valve cover gaskets, cylinder base gaskets, and side cover gaskets harden, shrink, and crack. The engine has a thin film of oil on every horizontal surface. It's not catastrophic but it's messy and attracts dirt that accelerates other wear.
causes: Gasket material deterioration from 40+ years of heat cycling
Cover bolt torque loss from vibration
Gasket surface corrosion preventing seal
Previous assembly with wrong gasket or no sealant
fix_procedure: 1. Identify leak sources: clean the engine thoroughly with degreaser, ride, then inspect for fresh oil. 2. Most common leaks: valve cover gasket (top of head), cylinder base gasket (bottom of cylinders), points cover O-ring, oil filter cover O-ring. 3. Replace gaskets with Vesrah or Cometic sets. 4. Clean gasket surfaces thoroughly — scrape old gasket material, use gasket remover. 5. Apply thin coat of Hylomar sealant to gasket surfaces (not Permatex/RTV — it squeezes out and clogs oil passages). 6. Torque all cover bolts to spec in sequence. 7. Forum tip: buy a complete gasket set (Vesrah VG-189 or equivalent, $30-50) rather than chasing one leak at a time. If one gasket is 40 years old, they all are. A complete re-gasket of the top end and covers takes a Saturday but eliminates all the weeping at once. Do NOT use RTV silicone on vintage Honda engines — it breaks apart and clogs the oil passages in the head. Hylomar Blue is the correct sealant — it stays put and doesn't harden. Also, check the torque on all cover bolts annually — vibration backs them out slowly.
parts_needed: Torque wrench (in-lb range for small bolts)
